Dismayed, he negotiated with the Jews, submitted to their terms, and swore to observe all their rights. Having come to this agreement, he offered a sacrifice, and honored the sanctuary and the place with a generous donation.
text.NRSV: he got word that Philip, who had been left in charge of the government, had revolted in Antioch; he was dismayed, called in the Jews, yielded and swore to observe all their rights, settled with them and offered sacrifice, honoured the sanctuary, and showed generosity to the holy place.

text.DRB: He embraced Machabeus, and made him governor and prince from Ptolemais even to the Gerrenians.
text.KJV: And accepted well of Maccabeus, made him principall gouernor from Ptolemais vnto the Gerrhenians,
text.NABRE: He received Maccabeus, and left Hegemonides as governor of the territory from Ptolemais to the region of the Gerrhenes.
text.NRSV: He received Maccabeus, left Hegemonides as governor from Ptolemais to Gerar,

text.DRB: But after, as he came to Ptolemais the Ptolemaians took grievously the covenant of amity, being offended lest perhaps they would break their league.
text.KJV: Came to Ptolemais, the people there were grieued for the couenants: for they stormed because they would make their couenants voide.
text.NABRE: When he came to Ptolemais, the people of Ptolemais were angered by the peace treaty; in fact they were so indignant that they wanted to annul its provisions.
text.NRSV: and went to Ptolemais. The people of Ptolemais were indignant over the treaty; in fact they were so angry that they wanted to annul its terms.

text.DRB: Then Lysias went up to the judgement seat, and declared the reason, and appeased the people, and returned to Antioch: and in this manner the king's journey and return proceeded.
text.KJV: Lysias went vp to the iudgement seat, said as much as could be in defence of the cause, perswaded, pacified, made them well affected, returned to Antioch. Thus it went touching the kings comming and departing.
text.NABRE: But Lysias took the platform, defended the treaty as well as he could and won them over by persuasion. After calming them and gaining their goodwill, he returned to Antioch. That is the story of the king’s attack and withdrawal.
text.NRSV: Lysias took the public platform, made the best possible defence, convinced them, appeased them, gained their goodwill, and set out for Antioch. This is how the king’s attack and withdrawal turned out.

text.KJV: After three yeres was Iudas enformed that Demetrius the sonne of Seleucus hauing entred by the hauen of Tripolis with a great power and nauie,
text.NABRE: Chapter 14 Three years later, Judas and his companions learned that Demetrius, son of Seleucus, had sailed into the port of Tripolis with a powerful army and a fleet,
text.NRSV: Alcimus Speaks against Judas 14
text.DRB: But after the space of three years Judas understood, and they that were with him, that Demetrius the son of Seleucus with a strong multitude, & with ships was come up by the port of Tripolis to commodious places,
and held the countries against Antiochus, and his governor Lysias.
text.KJV: Had taken the countrey, and killed Antiochus, and Lysias his protectour.
text.NABRE: and that he had occupied the country, after doing away with Antiochus and his guardian Lysias.
text.NRSV: and had taken possession of the country, having made away with Antiochus and his guardian Lysias.

text.KJV: Now one Alcimus who had beene hie Priest, and had defiled himselfe wilfully in the times of their mingling (with the Gentiles) seeing that by no meanes hee could saue himselfe, nor haue any more accesse to the holy Altar,
text.NABRE: A certain Alcimus, a former high priest, who had willfully incurred defilement before the time of the revolt, realized that there was no way for him to be safe and regain access to the holy altar.
text.NRSV: Now a certain Alcimus, who had formerly been high priest but had wilfully defiled himself in the times of separation, realized that there was no way for him to be safe or to have access again to the holy altar,
text.DRB: And one Alcimus that had been highpriest, but voluntarily was contaminated in the time of the confusion, considering that there was safety for him by no means, nor access to the altar,
came to king Demetrius in the year an hundred fifty, offering him a crown of gold, & a palm, & besides these, Talloes, which seemed to be of the temple. And that same day indeed he held his peace.
text.KJV: Came to king Demetrius in the hundreth and one and fiftieth yeere, presenting vnto him a crowne of golde, and a palme, and also of the boughes which were vsed solemnly in the Temple: and so that day he helde his peace.
text.NABRE: So he went to King Demetrius around the one hundred and fifty-first year and presented him with a gold crown and a palm branch, as well as some of the customary olive branches from the temple. On that day he kept quiet.
text.NRSV: and went to King Demetrius in about the one hundred and fifty-first year, presenting to him a crown of gold and a palm, and besides these some of the customary olive branches from the temple. During that day he kept quiet.

text.DRB: But having gotten a commodious time for his madness, being called of Demetrius to counsel, and asked on what thing and counsels the Jews rested, he answered:
text.KJV: Howbeit hauing gotten opportunity to further his foolish enterprise, being called into counsel by Demetrius, & asked how the Iewes stood affected, and what they intēded, he answered therunto;
text.NABRE: But he found an opportunity to further his mad scheme when he was invited to the council by Demetrius and questioned about the dispositions and intentions of the Jews. He replied:
text.NRSV: But he found an opportunity that furthered his mad purpose when he was invited by Demetrius to a meeting of the council and was asked about the attitude and intentions of the Jews. He answered:

text.DRB: They of the Jews that are called Assidians, of whom Judas Machabeus is captain, nourish battles, and move seditions, neither do they suffer the realm to be quiet.
text.KJV: Those of the Iewes that bee called Asideans (whose captaine is Iudas Maccabeus) nourish warre, and are seditious; and will not let the realme be in peace.
text.NABRE: “Those Jews called Hasideans, led by Judas Maccabeus, are warmongers, who stir up sedition and keep the kingdom from enjoying peace.
text.NRSV: ‘Those of the Jews who are called Hasideans, whose leader is Judas Maccabeus, are keeping up war and stirring up sedition, and will not let the kingdom attain tranquillity.

text.DRB: For I also being defrauded of my ancestors' glory (I mean of the high priesthood) am come hither:
text.KJV: Therfore I being depriued of mine ancestors honor (I meane the hie Priesthood) am now come hither.
text.NABRE: For this reason, now that I am deprived of my ancestral dignity, that is to say, the high priesthood, I have come here,
text.NRSV: Therefore I have laid aside my ancestral glory—I mean the high-priesthood—and have now come here,

text.DRB: principally indeed keeping fidelity to the king's commodities, but secondly also providing for my citizens, for by their naughtiness all our stock is not a little vexed.
text.KJV: First verily for the vnfained care I haue of things pertaining to the king, and secondly, euen for that I intend the good of mine owne countrey men: for all our nation is in no small misery, through the vnaduised dealing of them aforesaid.
text.NABRE: first, out of my genuine concern for the king’s interests, and second, out of consideration for my own compatriots, since our entire nation is suffering no little affliction from the rash conduct of the people just mentioned.
text.NRSV: first because I am genuinely concerned for the interests of the king, and second because I have regard also for my compatriots. For through the folly of those whom I have mentioned our whole nation is now in no small misfortune.

text.DRB: But all these things being known, o king, provide I pray thee, both for the country, and for our stock according to thy humanity published to all men.
text.KJV: Wherefore, O king, seeing thou knowest all these things, bee carefull for the countrey, and our nation, which is pressed on euery side, according to the clemency that thou readily shewest vnto all.
text.NABRE: When you have informed yourself in detail on these matters, O king, provide for our country and its hard-pressed people with the same gracious consideration that you show toward all.
text.NRSV: Since you are acquainted, O king, with the details of this matter, may it please you to take thought for our country and our hard-pressed nation with the gracious kindness that you show to all.

text.DRB: For as long as Judas is alive, it is unpossible that there be peace to the affairs.
text.KJV: For as long as Iudas liueth, it is not possible that the state should be quiet.
text.NABRE: As long as Judas is around, it is impossible for the government to enjoy peace.”
text.NRSV: For as long as Judas lives, it is impossible for the government to find peace.’

text.DRB: And such things being said of him, the other friends also behaving themselves as enemies against Judas, incensed Demetrius.
text.KJV: This was no sooner spoken of him, but others of the kings friends being malitiously set against Iudas, did more incense Demetrius.
text.NABRE: When he had said this, the other Friends who were hostile to Judas quickly added fuel to Demetrius’ indignation.
text.NRSV: When he had said this, the rest of the king’s Friends, who were hostile to Judas, quickly inflamed Demetrius still more.

text.KJV: And foorthwith calling Nicanor, who had bene master of the Elephants, and making him gouernour ouer Iudea, he sent him forth,
text.NABRE: Dealings with Nicanor. The king immediately chose Nicanor, who had been in command of the elephants, and appointed him governor of Judea. He sent him off
text.NRSV: He immediately chose Nicanor, who had been in command of the elephants, appointed him governor of Judea, and sent him off
text.DRB: Who forthwith sent Nicanor, chief over the elephants captain into Jurie:

text.KJV: Cōmanding him to slay Iudas, & to scatter them that were him, & to make Alcimus high priest of the great Temple.
text.NABRE: with orders to put Judas to death, to disperse his followers, and to set up Alcimus as high priest of the great temple.
text.NRSV: with orders to kill Judas and scatter his troops, and to instal Alcimus as high priest of the great temple.
text.DRB: giving him commission, that he should take Judas himself: but them that were with him, he should disperse, & make Alcimus the high priest of the greatest temple.
Then the nations, which had fled from Judas out of Jurie, joined themselves by troops with Nicanor, esteeming the miseries and calamities of the Jews the prosperities of their own affairs.
text.KJV: Then the heathen that had fled out of Iudea from Iudas, came to Nicanor by flocks, thinking the harme and calamities of the Iewes, to be their well-fare.
text.NABRE: The Gentiles from Judea, who had fled before Judas, flocked to Nicanor, thinking that the misfortunes and calamities of the Jews would mean prosperity for themselves.
text.NRSV: And the Gentiles throughout Judea, who had fled before Judas, flocked to join Nicanor, thinking that the misfortunes and calamities of the Jews would mean prosperity for themselves.

text.DRB: The Jews therefore having heard of Nicanor's coming, and the assembly of the nations, being sprinkled with earth besought him, that ordained his people to keep them for ever, & that protecteth his portion by evident signs.
text.KJV: Now when the Iewes heard of Nicanors comming, and that the heathen were vp against them, they cast earth vpon their heads, and made supplication to him that had stablished his people for euer, and who alwayes helpeth his portion with manifestation of his presence.
text.NABRE: When the Jews heard of Nicanor’s coming, and that the Gentiles were rallying to him, they sprinkled themselves with earth and prayed to him who established his people forever, and who always comes to the aid of his heritage by manifesting himself.
text.NRSV: Nicanor Makes Friends with Judas When the Jews heard of Nicanor’s coming and the gathering of the Gentiles, they sprinkled dust on their heads and prayed to him who established his own people for ever and always upholds his own heritage by manifesting himself.

text.DRB: And the captain commanding forthwith they removed from thence, and they came together to the castle of Dessau.
text.KJV: So at the commandement of the captaine, they remooued straightwayes from thence, and came neere vnto them, at the towne of Deffaro.
text.NABRE: At their leader’s command, they set out at once from there and came upon the enemy at the village of Adasa.
text.NRSV: At the command of the leader, they set out from there immediately and engaged them in battle at a village called Dessau.

text.DRB: And Simon the brother of Judas had joined battle with Nicanor: but he was made afraid with the sudden coming of the adversaries.
text.KJV: Now Simon, Iudas brother, had ioyned battell with Nicanor, but was somewhat discomfited, through the suddaine silence of his enemies.
text.NABRE: Judas’ brother Simon had engaged Nicanor, but he suffered a slight setback because of the sudden appearance of the enemy.
text.NRSV: Simon, the brother of Judas, had encountered Nicanor, but had been temporarily checked because of the sudden consternation created by the enemy.

text.DRB: Nevertheless Nicanor hearing the manliness of Judas' companions, & greatness of courage, that they had for the conflicts of their country, was afraid to make trial by blood.
text.KJV: Neuerthelesse Nicanor hearing of the manlinesse of them that were with Iudas, and the courageousnes that they had to fight for their countrey, durst not try the matter by the sword.
text.NABRE: However, when Nicanor heard of the valor of Judas and his companions, and the great courage with which they fought for their country, he shrank from deciding the issue by bloodshed.
text.NRSV: Nevertheless Nicanor, hearing of the valour of Judas and his troops and their courage in battle for their country, shrank from deciding the issue by bloodshed.

text.DRB: Wherefore he sent Posidonius, and Theodotius, and Matthias before to give and take the right hands.
text.KJV: Wherefore he sent Posidonius, and Theodotus, & Mattathias to make peace.
text.NABRE: So he sent Posidonius, Theodotus and Mattathias to exchange pledges of friendship.
text.NRSV: Therefore he sent Posidonius, Theodotus, and Mattathias to give and receive pledges of friendship.

text.DRB: And when there was long consultation of these things, and the captain himself had moved it to the multitude, there was one sentence of them all to accord unto amity.
text.KJV: So when they had taken long aduisement thereupon, and the captaine had made þ multitude acquainted therewith, and it appeared that they were all of one minde, they consented to the couenants,
text.NABRE: After a long discussion of the terms, each leader communicated them to his troops; and when general agreement was expressed, they assented to the treaty.
text.NRSV: When the terms had been fully considered, and the leader had informed the people, and it had appeared that they were of one mind, they agreed to the covenant.

text.DRB: Therefore they appointed a day, wherein they might secretly deal among themselves, and seats were brought forth & set for every one.
text.KJV: And appointed a day to meet in together by themselues, & when the day came, and stooles were set for either of them,
text.NABRE: A day was set on which the leaders would meet by themselves. From each side a chariot came forward, and thrones were set in place.
text.NRSV: The leaders set a day on which to meet by themselves. A chariot came forward from each army; seats of honour were set in place;

text.DRB: But Judas commanded armed men to be in places convenient, lest perhaps some mischief might suddenly arise from the enemies: and they made agreeable communication.
text.KJV: Iudas placed armed men ready in conuenient places, lest some treachery should bee suddenly practised by the enemies; so they made a peaceable cōference.
text.NABRE: Judas had posted armed men in readiness at strategic points for fear that the enemy might suddenly commit some treachery. But the conference was held in the proper way.
text.NRSV: Judas posted armed men in readiness at key places to prevent sudden treachery on the part of the enemy; so they duly held the consultation.

text.DRB: Nicanor abode at Jerusalem, and did nothing unjustly, and the flocks of the multitudes, that had been gathered together he diminished.
text.KJV: Now Nicanor abode in Ierusalem, and did no hurt, but sent away the people that came flocking vnto him.
text.NABRE: Nicanor stayed on in Jerusalem, where he did nothing out of place. He disbanded the throngs of people who gathered around him;
text.NRSV: Nicanor stayed on in Jerusalem and did nothing out of the way, but dismissed the flocks of people that had gathered.

text.DRB: And he esteemed Judas always dear from the heart, and he was inclined to the man.
text.KJV: And hee would not willingly haue Iudas out of his sight: for hee loued the man from his heart.
text.NABRE: and he always kept Judas in his company, for he felt affection for the man.
text.NRSV: And he kept Judas always in his presence; he was warmly attached to the man.

text.DRB: And he desired him to marry a wife, and to beget children. He made a marriage, he lived quietly, and they lived in common.
text.KJV: He praied him also to take a wife, and to beget children: so he maried, was quiet, and tooke part of this life.
text.NABRE: He urged him to marry and have children; so Judas married and settled into an ordinary life.
text.NRSV: He urged him to marry and have children; so Judas married, settled down, and shared the common life.

text.DRB: But Alcimus seeing their charity one towards another, and the covenants, came to Demetrius, and said that Nicanor assented to foreign matters, and that he meant to make Judas being a traitor to the kingdom, his successor.
text.KJV: But Alcimus perceiuing the loue that was betwixt them, and considering the couenants that were made, came to Demetrius, and tolde him that Nicanor was not well affected towards the state, for that he had ordained Iudas, a traitor to his realme, to be the kings successour.
text.NABRE: Nicanor’s Threat Against Judas. When Alcimus saw their mutual goodwill, he took the treaty that had been made, went to Demetrius, and said that Nicanor was plotting against the government, for he had appointed Judas, that conspirator against the kingdom, as his successor.
text.NRSV: Nicanor Turns against Judas But when Alcimus noticed their goodwill for one another, he took the covenant that had been made and went to Demetrius. He told him that Nicanor was disloyal to the government, since he had appointed that conspirator against the kingdom, Judas, to be his successor.

text.DRB: Therefore the king being exasperated with this man's most wicked criminations, wrote to Nicanor, saying, that he indeed was greatly displeased for the covenant of their amity: nevertheless that he commanded him to send Machabeus quickly prisoner to Antioch.
text.KJV: Then the king being in a rage, and prouoked with the accusations of the most wicked man, wrote to Nicanor, signifying that he was much displeased with the couenants, and commaunding him that hee should send Maccabeus prisoner in all haste vnto Antioch.
text.NABRE: Stirred up by the villain’s slander, the king became enraged. He wrote to Nicanor, stating that he was displeased with the treaty, and ordering him to send Maccabeus at once as a prisoner to Antioch.
text.NRSV: The king became excited and, provoked by the false accusations of that depraved man, wrote to Nicanor, stating that he was displeased with the covenant and commanding him to send Maccabeus to Antioch as a prisoner without delay.

text.DRB: Which things being known Nicanor was amazed, and took it grievously, if he should undo those things which they had covenanted, being nothing hurt of the man.
text.KJV: When this came to Nicanors hearing, he was much cōfounded in himselfe, and tooke it grieuously, that hee should make voyd the articles which were agreed vpon, the man being in no fault.
text.NABRE: When this message reached Nicanor he was dismayed and troubled at the thought of annulling his agreement with a man who had done no wrong.
text.NRSV: When this message came to Nicanor, he was troubled and grieved that he had to annul their agreement when the man had done no wrong.

text.DRB: But because he could not resist the king, he observed opportunity whereby to accomplish the commandment.
text.KJV: But because there was no dealing against the king, hee watched his time to accomplish this thing by pollicie.
text.NABRE: However, there was no way of opposing the king, so he watched for an opportunity to carry out this order by a stratagem.
text.NRSV: Since it was not possible to oppose the king, he watched for an opportunity to accomplish this by a stratagem.

text.DRB: But Machabeus seeing that Nicanor dealt with him more austerely, and that he exhibited his accustomed meeting more sternly, understanding this austerity not to be of good, a few of his company gathered together, he hid himself from Nicanor.
text.KJV: Notwithstāding when Maccabeus saw that Nicanor began to bee churlish vnto him, and that he entreated him more roughly then he was wont, perceiuing þ such sowre behauiour came not of good, hee gathered together not a few of his men, and withdrew himselfe frō Nicanor.
text.NABRE: But Maccabeus, noticing that Nicanor was more harsh in his dealings with him, and acting with unaccustomed rudeness when they met, concluded that this harshness was not a good sign. So he gathered together not a few of his men, and went into hiding from Nicanor.
text.NRSV: But Maccabeus, noticing that Nicanor was more austere in his dealings with him and was meeting him more rudely than had been his custom, concluded that this austerity did not spring from the best motives. So he gathered not a few of his men, and went into hiding from Nicanor.

text.DRB: Which when he understood that he was stoutly prevented of the man, he came to the most great and most holy temple: and the Priests offering the accustomed hosts, he commanded the man to be delivered unto him.
text.KJV: But the other knowing that he was notably preuented by Iudas policie, came into the great and holy Temple, and commanded the Priestes that were offering their vsual sacrifices, to deliuer him þ man.
text.NABRE: When Nicanor realized that he had been cleverly outwitted by the man, he went to the great and holy temple, at a time when the priests were offering the customary sacrifices, and ordered them to surrender Judas.
text.NRSV: When the latter became aware that he had been cleverly outwitted by the man, he went to the great and holy temple while the priests were offering the customary sacrifices, and commanded them to hand the man over.

text.DRB: Who saying with an oath, that they knew not where he was, that was demanded, stretching out his hand to the temple,
text.KJV: And whē they sware that they could not tel where þ man was, whō he sought,
text.NABRE: As they declared under oath that they did not know where the man they sought was,
text.NRSV: When they declared on oath that they did not know where the man was whom he wanted,

text.DRB: he sware, saying: Unless you will deliver Judas prisoner unto me, I will beat down this temple of God to the flat ground, and will dig down the altar, and this temple I will consecrate to Liber pater.
text.KJV: Hee stretched out his right hand toward the Temple, & made an oath in this maner: If you wil not deliuer me Iudas as a prisoner, I will lay this Temple of God euen with the ground, and I will breake downe the Altar, and erect a notable temple vnto Bacchus.
text.NABRE: he stretched out his right arm toward the temple and swore this oath: “If you do not hand Judas over to me as prisoner, I will level this shrine of God to the ground; I will tear down the altar, and erect here a splendid temple to Dionysus.”
text.NRSV: he stretched out his right hand towards the sanctuary, and swore this oath: ‘If you do not hand Judas over to me as a prisoner, I will level this shrine of God to the ground and tear down the altar, and build here a splendid temple to Dionysus.’

text.DRB: And when he had said these things, he departed. But the Priests stretching forth their hands unto heaven, invocated him that was always the defender of their nation, saying thus:
text.KJV: After these words he departed; then the Priests lift vp their handes towards heauen, & besought him þ was euer a defēder of their nation, saying in this maner:
text.NABRE: With these words he went away. The priests stretched out their hands toward heaven, calling upon the unfailing defender of our nation in these words:
text.NRSV: Having said this, he went away. Then the priests stretched out their hands towards heaven and called upon the constant Defender of our nation, in these words:

text.DRB: Thou o Lord of all, which lackest nothing, wouldest a temple of thy habitation to be made among us.
text.KJV: Thou, O Lord of all things, who hast neede of nothing, wast pleased that the Temple of thine habitation should be among vs.
text.NABRE: “Lord of all, though you are in need of nothing, you were pleased to have a temple for your dwelling place among us.
text.NRSV: ‘O Lord of all, though you have need of nothing, you were pleased that there should be a temple for your habitation among us;

text.DRB: And now o Lord holy of all holies, preserve for ever this house impolluted, which of late hath been cleansed.
text.KJV: Therefore now, O holy Lord of all holinesse, keepe this house euer vndefiled, which lately was cleansed, and stop euery vnrighteous mouth.
text.NABRE: Therefore, Holy One, Lord of all holiness, preserve forever undefiled this house, which has been so recently purified.” Martyrdom of Razis.
text.NRSV: so now, O holy One, Lord of all holiness, keep undefiled for ever this house that has been so recently purified.’

text.DRB: And Razias one of the ancients of Jerusalem, was accused to Nicanor, a man that was a lover of the city, and well reported of, who for his affection was called father of the Jews.
text.KJV: Now was there accused vnto Nicanor, one Razis, one of the Elders of Ierusalem, a louer of his countrey men, and a man of very good report, who for his kindnesse was called a father of þ Iewes.
text.NABRE: A certain Razis, one of the elders of Jerusalem, was denounced to Nicanor as a patriot. A man highly regarded, he was called a father of the Jews because of his goodwill toward them.
text.NRSV: Razis Dies for His Country A certain Razis, one of the elders of Jerusalem, was denounced to Nicanor as a man who loved his compatriots and was very well thought of and for his goodwill was called father of the Jews.

text.DRB: This man long time kept the purpose of continency in Judaism, and content to give his body and life for perseverance.
text.KJV: For in the former times, when they mingled not themselues with the Gentiles, he had bin accused of Iudaisme, and did boldly ieopard his body and life with al vehemency for the religion of þ Iewes.
text.NABRE: In the days before the revolt, he had been convicted of being a Jew, and had risked body and soul in his ardent zeal for Judaism.
text.NRSV: In former times, when there was no mingling with the Gentiles, he had been accused of Judaism, and he had most zealously risked body and life for Judaism.

text.DRB: But Nicanor willing to manifest the hatred that he had against the Jews, sent five hundred soldiers to take him.
text.KJV: So Nicanor willing to declare the hate that he bare vnto the Iewes, sent aboue fiue hūdred men of war to take him.
text.NABRE: Nicanor, to show his disdain for the Jews, sent more than five hundred soldiers to arrest him.
text.NRSV: Nicanor, wishing to exhibit the enmity that he had for the Jews, sent more than five hundred soldiers to arrest him;

text.DRB: For he thought if he had entrapped him, that he should do the Jews very great hurt.
text.KJV: For he thought by taking him to do much hurt.
text.NABRE: He thought that by arresting that man he would deal the Jews a hard blow.
text.NRSV: for he thought that by arresting him he would do them an injury.

text.DRB: But the multitudes coveting to rush into his house, and to break open the gate, and to set fire thereto, when he was in taking, he struck himself with a sword:
text.KJV: Now when the multitude would haue taken the towre, and violently broken into the vtter doore, and bade that fire should be brought to burne it, he being ready to be taken on euery side, fell vpon his sword,
text.NABRE: But when the troops, on the point of capturing the tower, were forcing the outer gate and calling for fire to set the door ablaze, Razis, now caught on all sides, turned his sword against himself,
text.NRSV: When the troops were about to capture the tower and were forcing the door of the courtyard, they ordered that fire be brought and the doors burned. Being surrounded, Razis fell upon his own sword,

text.DRB: choosing to die nobly rather than to be made subject to sinners, and against his noble birth to suffer unworthy injuries.
text.KJV: Chusing rather to die manfully, then to come into the hands of the wicked to be abused otherwise then beseemed his noble birth.
text.NABRE: preferring to die nobly rather than fall into the hands of vile men and suffer outrages unworthy of his noble birth.
text.NRSV: preferring to die nobly rather than to fall into the hands of sinners and suffer outrages unworthy of his noble birth.

text.DRB: But whereas for haste he had not made the wound with a sure struck, and the multitudes brake in within the doors, running back boldly to the wall, he threw down himself manfully headlong unto the multitudes:
text.KJV: But missing his stroke through haste, the multitude also rushing within the doores, he ran boldly vp to the wall, and cast himselfe downe manfully among the thickest of them.
text.NABRE: In the excitement of the struggle he failed to strike exactly. So while the troops rushed in through the doors, he gallantly ran up to the top of the wall and courageously threw himself down into the crowd.
text.NRSV: But in the heat of the struggle he did not hit exactly, and the crowd was now rushing in through the doors. He courageously ran up on the wall, and bravely threw himself down into the crowd.

text.DRB: who quickly giving place to his fall he fell upon his neck.
text.KJV: But they quickly giuing backe, and a space being made, he fell downe into the midst of the void place.
text.NABRE: But as they quickly drew back and left an opening, he fell into the middle of the empty space.
text.NRSV: But as they quickly drew back, a space opened and he fell in the middle of the empty space.

text.DRB: And when he had breathed, incensed in mind he arose: and when his blood ran with a great stream, and he was wounded with most grievous wounds, running he passed through the multitude:
text.KJV: Neuerthelesse while there was yet breath within him, being inflamed with anger, he rose vp, and though his blood gushed out like spouts of water, and his wounds were grieuous, yet hee ranne through the midst of the throng, and standing vpon a steepe rocke,
text.NABRE: Still breathing, and inflamed with anger, he got up and ran through the crowd, with blood gushing from his frightful wounds. Then, standing on a steep rock,
text.NRSV: Still alive and aflame with anger, he rose, and though his blood gushed forth and his wounds were severe he ran through the crowd; and standing upon a steep rock,

text.DRB: and standing upon a certain steep rock, and now being become without blood, griping his bowels, with both hands he cast them upon multitudes, invocating the dominatour of life and spirit, that he would restore these to him again: and so he departed this life.
text.KJV: When as his blood was now quite gone, hee pluckt out his bowels, & taking them in both his hands, hee cast them vpon the throng, and calling vpon the Lord of life and spirit to restore him those againe, he thus died.
text.NABRE: as he lost the last of his blood, he tore out his entrails and flung them with both hands into the crowd, calling upon the Lord of life and of spirit to give these back to him again. Such was the manner of his death.
text.NRSV: with his blood now completely drained from him, he tore out his entrails, took them in both hands, and hurled them at the crowd, calling upon the Lord of life and spirit to give them back to him again. This was the manner of his death.

text.KJV: But Nicanor hearing that Iudas and his company were in the strong places about Samaria, resolued without any danger to set vpon them on þ sabbath day.
text.NABRE: Chapter 15 Nicanor’s Arrogance. When Nicanor learned that Judas and his companions were in the territory of Samaria, he decided he could attack them in complete safety on the day of rest.
text.NRSV: Nicanor’s Arrogance 15
text.DRB: But Nicanor as he understood that Judas was in the places of Samaria, he purposed with all violence to join battle the day of the Sabbath.
But the Jews that of necessity followed him, saying do not so fiercely and barbarously, but give honour to the day of the sanctification, and honour him that beholdeth all things:
text.KJV: Neuertheles, the Iewes that were compelled to go with him, said, O destroy not so cruelly and barbarously, but giue honour to that day, which he that seeth all things, hath honoured with holinesse aboue
text.NABRE: The Jews who were forced to accompany him pleaded, “Do not massacre them so savagely and barbarously, but show respect for the day which the All-seeing has exalted with holiness above all other days.”
text.NRSV: When the Jews who were compelled to follow him said, ‘Do not destroy so savagely and barbarously, but show respect for the day that he who sees all things has honoured and hallowed above other days’,

text.DRB: that unhappy man asked, if there were a power in heaven, that commanded the Sabbath day to be kept.
text.KJV: Then this most vngracious wretch demanded, if there were a mightie one in heauen that had commanded the Sabbath day to be kept.
text.NABRE: At this the thrice-accursed wretch asked if there was a ruler in heaven who prescribed the keeping of the sabbath day.
text.NRSV: the thrice-accursed wretch asked if there were a sovereign in heaven who had commanded the keeping of the sabbath day.

text.DRB: And they answering: There is the living Lord himself in heaven, the potent, that commanded the seventh day to be kept.
text.KJV: And when they said, There is in heauen a liuing Lord, and mightie, who commanded the seuenth day to be kept,
text.NABRE: They replied, “It is the living Lord, the ruler in heaven, who commands the observance of the sabbath day.”
text.NRSV: When they declared, ‘It is the living Lord himself, the Sovereign in heaven, who ordered us to observe the seventh day,’

text.DRB: But he said. And I am potent upon the earth, that commanded arms to be taken, and the king's affairs to be accomplished. Nevertheless he obtained not to accomplish his counsel.
text.KJV: Then said the other, And I also am mightie vpon earth, & I cōmand to take armes, and to do the kings busines: yet he obteined not to haue his wicked wil done.
text.NABRE: Then he said, “I, the ruler on earth, command you to take up arms and carry out the king’s business.” Nevertheless he did not succeed in carrying out his cruel plan.
text.NRSV: he replied, ‘But I am a sovereign also, on earth, and I command you to take up arms and finish the king’s business.’ Nevertheless, he did not succeed in carrying out his abominable design.

text.DRB: And Nicanor indeed puffed up with exceeding pride, had thought to have set up a common victorious memory of Judas.
text.KJV: So Nicanor in exceeding pride and haughtinesse, determined to set vp a publike moument of his victorie ouer Iudas, and them that were with him.
text.NABRE: In his utter boastfulness and arrogance Nicanor had determined to erect a public victory monument over Judas and his companions.
text.NRSV: Judas Prepares the Jews for Battle This Nicanor in his utter boastfulness and arrogance had determined to erect a public monument of victory over Judas and his forces.

text.DRB: But Machabeus always trusted with all hope that there would come aid from God to them.
text.KJV: But Maccabeus had euer sure confidence that the Lord would helpe him.
text.NABRE: But Maccabeus remained confident, fully convinced that he would receive help from the Lord.
text.NRSV: But Maccabeus did not cease to trust with all confidence that he would get help from the Lord.

text.DRB: And exhorted his company, that they should not fear at the coming of the nations, but should have in mind the aids given unto them from heaven, and now should hope that they should have the victory from the Almighty.
text.KJV: Wherfore he exhorted his people not to feare the comming of the heathen against them, but to remember the helpe which in former times they had receiued from heauen, and now to expect the victory, and aid which should come vnto them from the Almightie.
text.NABRE: He urged his men not to fear the attack of the Gentiles, but mindful of the help they had received in the past from Heaven, to expect now the victory that would be given them by the Almighty.
text.NRSV: He exhorted his troops not to fear the attack of the Gentiles, but to keep in mind the former times when help had come to them from heaven, and so to look for the victory that the Almighty would give them.

text.DRB: And speaking unto them out of the law, and the Prophets, admonishing them also of the conflicts, that they had made before, he made them the more prompt:
text.KJV: And so comforting them out of the law, and the prophets, and withall putting them in mind of the battels that they won afore, he made them more cheerefull.
text.NABRE: By encouraging them with words from the law and the prophets, and by reminding them of the battles they had already won, he filled them with fresh enthusiasm.
text.NRSV: Encouraging them from the law and the prophets, and reminding them also of the struggles they had won, he made them the more eager.

text.DRB: and so their hearts being encouraged, withal he shewed the fraud of the Gentiles, and their breaking of oaths.
text.KJV: And when he had stirred vp their minds, he gaue them their charge, shewing them therewithall the falshood of the heathen, and the breach of othes.
text.NABRE: Having stirred up their courage, he gave his orders and pointed out at the same time the perfidy of the Gentiles and their violation of oaths.
text.NRSV: When he had aroused their courage, he issued his orders, at the same time pointing out the perfidy of the Gentiles and their violation of oaths.

text.DRB: And he armed every one of them not with fence of buckler and spear, but with very good words, and exhortations, declaring a dream worthy to be credited, whereby he rejoiced them all.
text.KJV: Thus he armed euery one of them not so much with defence of shields and speares, as with comfortable and good words: and besides that, he tolde them a dreame worthy to be beleeued, as if it had bin so indeed, which did not a litle reioyce them.
text.NABRE: When he had armed each of them, not so much with the security of shield and spear as with the encouragement of noble words, he cheered them all by relating a dream, a kind of waking vision, worthy of belief.
text.NRSV: He armed each of them not so much with confidence in shields and spears as with the inspiration of brave words, and he cheered them all by relating a dream, a sort of vision, which was worthy of belief.

text.KJV: And this was his vision: that Onias, who had bin high Priest, a vertuous, and a good man, reuerend in conuersation, gentle in condition, well spoken also, and exercised from a child in all points of vertue, holding vp his hands, prayed for the whole bodie of the Iewes.
text.NABRE: What he saw was this: Onias, the former high priest, a noble and good man, modest in bearing, gentle in manner, distinguished in speech, and trained from childhood in all that belongs to excellence, was praying with outstretched arms for the whole Jewish community.
text.NRSV: What he saw was this: Onias, who had been high priest, a noble and good man, of modest bearing and gentle manner, one who spoke fittingly and had been trained from childhood in all that belongs to excellence, was praying with outstretched hands for the whole body of the Jews.
text.DRB: And the vision was in this manner: Onias who had been the high-Priest, a good and benign man, reverent to behold, modest of manners, and comely of speech, and who from a child was exercised in virtues, that he stretching forth the hands prayed for all the people of the Jews.

text.KJV: This done, in like maner there appeared a man with gray haires, & exceeding glorious, who was of a wonderfull and excellent maiestie.
text.NABRE: Then in the same way another man appeared, distinguished by his white hair and dignity, and with an air of wondrous and majestic authority.
text.NRSV: Then in the same fashion another appeared, distinguished by his grey hair and dignity, and of marvellous majesty and authority.
text.DRB: After this that there appeared also another man marvelous for age, and glory, and for the port of great dignity about him.

text.KJV: Then Onias answered, saying, This is a louer of the brethren, who prayeth much for the people, and for the holy citie, (to wit) Ieremias þ prophet of God.
text.NABRE: Onias then said of him, “This is a man who loves his fellow Jews and fervently prays for the people and the holy city—the prophet of God, Jeremiah.”
text.NRSV: And Onias spoke, saying, ‘This is a man who loves the family of Israel and prays much for the people and the holy city—Jeremiah, the prophet of God.’
text.DRB: And that Onias answering said: This is a lover of his brethren, and of the people of Israel: this is he that prayeth much for the people, and for the whole city, Jeremy the Prophet of God.

text.KJV: Whereupon Ieremias, holding forth his right hand, gaue to Iudas a sword of gold, and in giuing it spake thus:
text.NABRE: Stretching out his right hand, Jeremiah presented a gold sword to Judas. As he gave it to him he said,
text.NRSV: Jeremiah stretched out his right hand and gave to Judas a golden sword, and as he gave it he addressed him thus:
text.DRB: And that Jeremy put forth his right hand, and gave unto Judas a sword of gold, saying:
Take the holy sword a gift from God, wherewith thou shalt overthrow the adversaries of my people Israel.
text.KJV: Take this holy sword a gift from God, with the which thou shalt wound the aduersaries.
text.NABRE: “Accept this holy sword as a gift from God; with it you shall shatter your adversaries.”
text.NRSV: ‘Take this holy sword, a gift from God, with which you will strike down your adversaries.’

text.DRB: Being exhorted therefore with the words of Judas exceeding good, by which the courages might be stirred up, & the hearts of the young men strengthened, they resolved to fight, and to encounter manfully: that manhood might decide the matter, because the holy city, and the temple were in danger.
text.KJV: Thus being well comforted by the words of Iudas, which were very good, and able to stirre them vp to valour, and to encourage the hearts of the yong men, they determined not to pitch campe, but couragiously to set vpon them, and manfully to trie the matter by conflict, because the citie, and the Sanctuarie, and the Temple were in danger.
text.NABRE: Encouraged by Judas’ words, so noble and capable of instilling valor and stirring young hearts to courage, they determined not merely to march, but to charge gallantly and decide the issue by hand-to-hand combat with the utmost courage, since city, sanctuary and temple were in danger.
text.NRSV: Encouraged by the words of Judas, so noble and so effective in arousing valour and awaking courage in the souls of the young, they determined not to carry on a campaign but to attack bravely, and to decide the matter by fighting hand to hand with all courage, because the city and the sanctuary and the temple were in danger.

text.DRB: For there was less care for their wives, and children, and also for their brethren, and kinsmen: but the greatest and principal fear was for the holiness of the temple.
text.KJV: For the care that they tooke for their wiues, and their children, their brethren, and kinsfolkes, was in least account with them: but the greatest, and principall feare, was for the holy Temple.
text.NABRE: They were not so much concerned about wives and children, or family and relations; their first and foremost fear was for the consecrated sanctuary.
text.NRSV: Their concern for wives and children, and also for brothers and sisters and relatives, lay upon them less heavily; their greatest and first fear was for the consecrated sanctuary.

text.DRB: And they also that were in the city, took no little care for them that were to join battle.
text.KJV: Also they that were in the citie, tooke not the least care, being troubled for the conflict abroad.
text.NABRE: Those who were left in the city suffered no less an agony, anxious as they were about the battle in the open country.
text.NRSV: And those who had to remain in the city were in no little distress, being anxious over the encounter in the open country.

text.DRB: And when they did all hope that judgement would be given, and the enemies were present, and the army was set in array, the beasts & horsemen disposed in convenient place,
text.KJV: And now when as all looked what should bee þ triall, & the enemies were already come neere, and the armie was set in aray, and the beasts conueniently placed, and the horsemen set in wings:
text.NABRE: Everyone now awaited the decisive moment. The enemy were already drawing near with their troops drawn up in battle line, their beasts placed in strategic positions, and their cavalry stationed on the flanks.
text.NRSV: The Defeat and Death of Nicanor When all were now looking forward to the coming issue, and the enemy was already close at hand with their army drawn up for battle, the elephants strategically stationed and the cavalry deployed on the flanks,

text.DRB: Machabeus considering the coming of the multitude, and the variety of the provision of armour, and the fierceness of the beasts, stretching forth his hands unto heaven, he invocated our Lord, that worketh wonders, who not according to the might of arms, but according as it pleaseth him, giveth victory to the worthy.
text.KJV: Maccabeus seeing the comming of the multitude, and the diuers preparations of armour, and the fiercenesse of the beasts, stretched out his hands towards heauen, and called vpon the Lord, that worketh wonders, knowing that victorie commeth not by armes, but euen as it seemeth good to him, he giueth it to such as are worthy:
text.NABRE: Defeat of Nicanor. Maccabeus, surveying the hosts before him, the variety of weaponry, and the fierceness of their beasts, stretched out his hands toward heaven and called upon the Lord who works wonders; for he knew that it is not weapons but the Lord’s decision that brings victory to those who deserve it.
text.NRSV: Maccabeus, observing the masses that were in front of him and the varied supply of arms and the savagery of the elephants, stretched out his hands towards heaven and called upon the Lord who works wonders; for he knew that it is not by arms, but as the Lord decides, that he gains the victory for those who deserve it.

text.DRB: And he said invocating in this manner: Thou Lord which didst send thy Angel in the time of Ezechias king of Juda, and didst kill an hundred eighty five thousand of the camp of Sennacherib:
text.KJV: Therefore in his prayer he said after this maner: O Lord, thou diddest send thine Angel in the time of Ezekias king of Iudea, and diddest slay in the host of Sennacherib, an hundred, fourescore, and fiue thousand.
text.NABRE: Calling upon God, he spoke in this manner: “You, master, sent your angel in the days of King Hezekiah of Judea, and he slew a hundred and eighty-five thousand men of Sennacherib’s camp.
text.NRSV: He called upon him in these words: ‘O Lord, you sent your angel in the time of King Hezekiah of Judea, and he killed fully one hundred and eighty-five thousand in the camp of Sennacherib.

text.DRB: and now o Dominatour of the heavens send thy good Angel before us, in fear and trembling of the greatness of thine arm,
text.KJV: Wherfore now also O Lord of heauen, send a good Angel before vs, for a feare, and dread vnto them.
text.NABRE: And now, Sovereign of the heavens, send a good angel to spread fear and trembling ahead of us.
text.NRSV: So now, O Sovereign of the heavens, send a good angel to spread terror and trembling before us.

text.DRB: that they may be afraid, which with blasphemy come against thine holy people. And he indeed ended his prayer thus.
text.KJV: And through the might of thine arme, let those bee stricken with terror, that come against thy holy people to blaspheme. And he ended thus.
text.NABRE: By the might of your arm may those be struck down who have blasphemously come against your holy people!” With these words he ended his prayer.
text.NRSV: By the might of your arm may these blasphemers who come against your holy people be struck down.’ With these words he ended his prayer.

text.DRB: But Nicanor, and they that were with him, with trumpets & songs came near.
text.KJV: Then Nicanor, and they that were with him came forward with trumpets, and songs.
text.NABRE: Nicanor and his troops advanced to the sound of trumpets and battle songs.
text.NRSV: Nicanor and his troops advanced with trumpets and battle-songs,

text.DRB: But Judas, & they that were with him, invocating God by prayers joined battle:
text.KJV: But Iudas, and his company encountred the enemies with inuocation, and prayer.
text.NABRE: But Judas and his troops met the enemy with supplication and prayers.
text.NRSV: but Judas and his troops met the enemy in battle with invocations to God and prayers.

text.DRB: with the hand indeed fighting, but in their hearts praying to our Lord, they overthrew no less than five and thirty thousand, being greatly delighted with the presence of God.
text.KJV: So that fighting with their hands, and praying vnto God with their hearts, they slew no lesse then thirty and fiue thousand men: for through the appearance of God, they were greatly cheered.
text.NABRE: Fighting with their hands and praying to God with their hearts, they laid low at least thirty-five thousand, and rejoiced greatly over this manifestation of God’s power.
text.NRSV: So, fighting with their hands and praying to God in their hearts, they laid low at least thirty-five thousand, and were greatly gladdened by God’s manifestation.

text.KJV: Now when the battell was done, returning againe with ioy, they knew that Nicanor lay dead in his harnesse.
text.NABRE: When the battle was over and they were joyfully departing, they discovered Nicanor fallen there in all his armor;
text.NRSV: When the action was over and they were returning with joy, they recognized Nicanor, lying dead, in full armour.
text.DRB: And when they had ceased, and returned with joy, they understood that Nicanor was slain, for all his armour.
Ashout therefore being made, and a great cry, they blessed the Almighty Lord in their country language.
text.KJV: Then they made a great shout, and a noise, praising the Almighty in their owne language:
text.NABRE: so they raised tumultuous shouts in their ancestral language in praise of the divine Sovereign.
text.NRSV: Then there was shouting and tumult, and they blessed the Sovereign Lord in the language of their ancestors.

text.DRB: And Judas, who by all means was in body and mind ready to die for his citizens, commanded that Nicanor's head, & hand with the shoulder being cut off, should be carried to Jerusalem.
text.KJV: And Iudas, who was euer the chiefe defender of the citizens both in body, and minde, and who continued his loue towards his countrymen all his life, commanded to strike off Nicanors head, and his hand, with his shoulder, & bring them to Ierusalem.
text.NABRE: Then Judas, that man who was ever in body and soul the chief defender of his fellow citizens, and had maintained from youth his affection for his compatriots, ordered Nicanor’s head and right arm up to the shoulder to be cut off and taken to Jerusalem.
text.NRSV: Then the man who was ever in body and soul the defender of his people, the man who maintained his youthful goodwill towards his compatriots, ordered them to cut off Nicanor’s head and arm and carry them to Jerusalem.

text.DRB: Whither when he was come, having called his countrymen, and the Priests to the altar, he sent also for them that were in the castle.
text.KJV: So when he was there, and had called them of his nation together, and set the priests before the altar, he sent for them that were of the Towre,
text.NABRE: When he arrived there, he assembled his compatriots, stationed the priests before the altar, and sent for those in the citadel.
text.NRSV: When he arrived there and had called his compatriots together and stationed the priests before the altar, he sent for those who were in the citadel.

text.DRB: And shewing them the head of Nicanor, and the wicked hand, which he stretching forth against the holy house of Almighty God, had mightily bragged.
text.KJV: And shewed them vile Nicanors head, and the hand of that blasphemer, which with proud brags he had stretched out against the holy Temple of the Almightie.
text.NABRE: He showed them the vile Nicanor’s head and the wretched blasphemer’s arm that had been boastfully stretched out against the holy dwelling of the Almighty.
text.NRSV: He showed them the vile Nicanor’s head and that profane man’s arm, which had been boastfully stretched out against the holy house of the Almighty.

text.DRB: The tongue also of impious Nicanor being cut out, he commanded to be given piecemeal to the birds: and the hand of the furious man to be hanged up against the temple.
text.KJV: And when he had cut out the tongue of that vngodly Nicanor, he commanded that they should giue it by pieces vnto the foules, and hang vp the reward of his madnesse before the Temple.
text.NABRE: He cut out the tongue of the godless Nicanor, saying he would feed it piecemeal to the birds and would hang up the other wages of his folly opposite the temple.
text.NRSV: He cut out the tongue of the ungodly Nicanor and said that he would feed it piecemeal to the birds and would hang up these rewards of his folly opposite the sanctuary.

text.DRB: All therefore blessed the Lord of heaven, saying: Blessed be he, that hath kept his place undefiled.
text.KJV: So euery man praised towards the heauen the glorious Lord, saying, Blessed be hee that hath kept his owne place vndefiled.
text.NABRE: At this, everyone looked toward heaven and praised the Lord who manifests himself: “Blessed be the one who has preserved undefiled his own place!”
text.NRSV: And they all, looking to heaven, blessed the Lord who had manifested himself, saying, ‘Blessed is he who has kept his own place undefiled!’

text.DRB: And he hung up Nicanor's head in the top of the castle, that it might be an evident, and manifest sign of the help of God.
text.KJV: He hanged also Nicanors head vpon the Towre, an euident, and manifest signe vnto all, of the helpe of the Lord.
text.NABRE: Judas hung Nicanor’s head and arm on the wall of the citadel, a clear and evident sign to all of the Lord’s help.
text.NRSV: Judas hung Nicanor’s head from the citadel, a clear and conspicuous sign to everyone of the help of the Lord.

text.DRB: Therefore all by common counsel decreed, by no means to let pass this day without solemnity:
text.KJV: And they ordained all with a common decree, in no case to let that day passe without solemnitie: but to celebrate the thirteenth day of the twelfth moneth, which in the Syrian tongue is called Adar, the day before Mardocheus day.
text.NABRE: By public vote it was unanimously decreed never to let this day pass unobserved, but to celebrate the thirteenth day of the twelfth month, called Adar in Aramaic, the eve of Mordecai’s Day.
text.NRSV: And they all decreed by public vote never to let this day go unobserved, but to celebrate the thirteenth day of the twelfth month—which is called Adar in the Aramaic language—the day before Mordecai’s day.

text.KJV: Thus went it with Nicanor, and from that time forth, the Hebrewes had the citie in their power: and heere will I make an end.
text.NABRE: VII. Epilogue Compiler’s Apology. Since Nicanor’s doings ended in this way, with the city remaining in the possession of the Hebrews from that time on, I will bring my story to an end here too.
text.NRSV: This, then, is how matters turned out with Nicanor, and from that time the city has been in the possession of the Hebrews. So I will here end my story.
text.DRB: but to keep the solemnity the thirteenth day of the month Adar, which is called in the Syrian language, the day before Mardocheus' day.

text.KJV: And if I haue done well, and as is fitting the story, it is that which I desired: but if slenderly, and meanly, it is that which I could attaine vnto.
text.NABRE: If it is well written and to the point, that is what I wanted; if it is poorly done and mediocre, that is the best I could do.
text.NRSV: The Compiler’s Epilogue If it is well told and to the point, that is what I myself desired; if it is poorly done and mediocre, that was the best I could do.
text.DRB: These things therefore being done concerning Nicanor, and from that time the city being possessed of the Hebrews, I also in these will make an end of speaking.
And if well, and as is competent for a story, that myself also would: but if not so worthily it resteth to be pardoned me.
text.KJV: For as it is hurtfull to drinke wine, or water alone; & as wine mingled with water is pleasant, and delighteth the tast: euen so speech finely framed, delighteth the eares of them that read the storie. And heere shall be an end.
text.NABRE: Just as it is unpleasant to drink wine by itself or just water, whereas wine mixed with water makes a delightful and pleasing drink, so a skillfully composed story delights the ears of those who read the work. Let this, then, be the end.
text.NRSV: For just as it is harmful to drink wine alone, or, again, to drink water alone, while wine mixed with water is sweet and delicious and enhances one’s enjoyment, so also the style of the story delights the ears of those who read the work. And here will be the end.

text.DRB: For as to drink always wine, or always water is hurtful, but to use now one now another is delectable: so to the readers, if the speech be always exact, it will not be grateful. Here therefore it shall be ended.
